oai_dc:dc.dc:title: Carcass weight trends in fed cattle
oai_dc:dc.dc:creator: Shreck, Adam
oai_dc:dc.dc:subject: carcass weight
dressing percent
feeding margin
oai_dc:dc.dc:description: This past year, cattle have reached new all-time highs for prices and average slaughter weights. These increases in slaughter weights are not a new phenomenon. Fed cattle production in the United States and Canada have shown a continual increase in slaughter weights for the last 60 years. As fed cattle slaughter weights increase, carcass fatness, dressing percent and quality grade also increase. Conversely, increased slaughter weights worsen gain and feed efficiency, but to different magnitudes de­pending if determined on a live or carcass-adjusted basis, due to differing amounts of gain retained in the carcass. Increased slaughter weights are also a reflection of greater time on feed, leading to greater mortality from increased days at risk. Iden­tifying acceptable upper limits for fed cattle carcass weights is population dependent and includes considerations for animal welfare in addition to readily quantifiable changes in cost of gain relative to slaughter price received.
oai_dc:dc.dc:source: American Association of Bovine Practitioners Conference Proceedings; Vol. 58 No. 2 (2025): 2025 AABP Annual Conference Proceedings; 31-34
oai_dc:dc.dc:rights: Copyright (c) 2026 American Association of Bovine Practitioners
